%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand how MATLAB vector operations replace repetitive scalar computations, and learn to create and index row vectors using colon notation.

In B.7-3, we discover one of MATLAB's most powerful features: instead of computing one value at a time, a single expression can compute hundreds of values simultaneously. This is done by passing a **vector** — an ordered list of numbers — as the argument to a function.

You will learn to create evenly spaced row vectors using the `a:b:c` notation, apply functions to entire vectors at once, and access individual or multiple elements using indexing. After this section, you will be able to generate signal samples efficiently and understand why MATLAB indices start at 1 rather than 0.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 MATLAB colon notation `a:b:c` creates an evenly spaced row vector. Index 1 corresponds to the first element — not index 0.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215675469-4091.png)

## Core Idea

The central relationship in B.7-3 is this: **MATLAB's colon notation `a:b:c` creates a row vector**, and any function applied to that vector is evaluated at every element simultaneously.

The syntax works as follows:
- `a` — starting value
- `b` — step size (increment between consecutive elements)
- `c` — ending value (the vector stops at or before `c`)

For example, `0:1:4` produces `[0, 1, 2, 3, 4]`, and `0:2:8` produces `[0, 2, 4, 6, 8]`.

### KEY TRAP

MATLAB indices are **integers starting at 1**, not 0. If you define `k = 0:1:99`, then `k(5)` returns `4` — the fifth element is the value 4, not 5. The index and the stored value are different things.

> **Exam pattern:** You may be asked to identify which index retrieves a specific value of `k`, or to interpret `w(98:100)` as accessing the last three elements. Always distinguish the **index** (position in the vector) from the **value** stored at that position.

This vector-first approach is what makes MATLAB efficient for signal processing: one line replaces a loop over hundreds of samples.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Colon notation `a:b:c` creates an evenly spaced row vector in one expression.
- Functions applied to a vector compute all values simultaneously — no loop needed.
- MATLAB indices start at 1; the index and the stored variable value are not the same.

*In the next section, we will use these vector foundations to rapidly generate and plot signal waveforms, seeing how a single MATLAB expression can produce a complete time-domain signal.*
